cards.text: In {{c1::procedural programming}}, the focus is on creating functions that implement program logic, with {{c2::functions and data}} as separate entities.

cards.front: What are the two major components that real-world objects have?
cards.back:
• Properties (represented by data members)
  • Behaviors (represented by member functions)

cards.text: In object-oriented programming, the focus is on creating program-defined {{c1::data types}} that contain both {{c2::properties}} and {{c3::behaviors}}.

cards.question: Which code represents an OOP approach?
cards.explanation: In OOP, the subject (you) owns the behavior (eat), making it clearer who performs the action and what the accessory objects are.

cards.text:
To define a simple class-like structure with properties:
{{c1::struct Cat
{
    std::string_view name{"cat"};
    int numLegs{4};
};}}}

cards.front: What are the three main benefits of OOP over procedural programming?
cards.back:
• Easier modularization - properties and behaviors together
  • Higher code reusability
  • More intuitive way to work with data

cards.front: What are the four key concepts that OOP brings to the table?

cards.text: In traditional programming, an {{c1::object}} is a piece of memory to store values. In OOP, we prefer the term {{c2::class object}} to refer to objects that combine properties and behaviors.

cards.concept: Why OOP is better for code evolution and maintenance
cards.explanation: In OOP, when adding new types (like a Snake to an animal program), you only need to create a new type definition. In procedural programming, you must modify multiple existing functions (animalName(), numLegs(), etc.), touching more code and risking breakage.
examples: // Procedural: must modify AnimalType enum, animalName(), numLegs()
// OOP: just create struct Snake { ... }; and use it

cards.front: In procedural programming, why is modifying code to add a new type (like a snake animal) risky?
cards.back: You must modify multiple existing functions and the enum type definition. If the type is used in many places, that's a lot of code to touch and potentially break.

cards.question: What does this code print?
cards.code:
struct Cat { std::string_view name{"cat"}; int numLegs{4}; };
Cat animal;
std::cout << animal.name << " " << animal.numLegs;
cards.explanation: The Cat struct defines default values for name and numLegs. When instantiated, animal gets these default values.

cards.front: Why might you prefer OOP with separate structs (Cat, Dog, Chicken) over a common Animal struct when each animal has the same members?
cards.back: With separate structs, you can add members specific to one type (like wormsPerDay for Chicken) without affecting other types. A common Animal struct would force all animals to have every member.
